title: Token Cost Tracks What Comes Back Into Context, Not Where Code Ran — and Judgment Can't Be Delegated to a Function
problem: Victor asked whether pushing a declared intent through GitHub Actions and waiting for a webhook notification would save Claude tokens compared to verifying locally before committing, on the assumption that GitHub Actions' compute is free for Claude while local tool calls burn tokens. A related, separate question followed in the same exchange: whether Claude should rely on more pre-built 'blueprint' functions instead of 'recalculating with assumptions', framed as a related possible token-cost optimization.
exploration: traced that every tool call — a local Bash command or a GitHub Actions status/log fetch — costs tokens proportional to what's returned into Claude's context, not to where the computation physically ran; a terse local summary line and a compact CI status object cost about the same, while a verbose CI job log costs more than either

identified the real cost driver as iteration count on failure, not execution location: verifying locally before committing catches a mistake in one cheap round; pushing first and letting CI be the first check means a failure costs a full push-wait-notify-investigate-fix-push cycle, and CI logs are usually more verbose to read than Claude's own controlled local output

separately confirmed Claude already defaults to calling existing deterministic functions (lib/build-*.js's exported pure functions, applyIntent/validateIntent, checkKeyAlignment) rather than re-deriving equivalent logic through reasoning — mechanical work already runs at near-zero token cost regardless of local-vs-CI, so token cost is already concentrated on the one step that can't be delegated to a function: semantic judgment (e.g., reading a page's actual prose to decide which department it belongs to)
decision: No infrastructure change from this exchange. Named as a standing distinction for future sessions: (1) token cost tracks output volume and verbosity, not execution location — shifting a computation to CI doesn't create savings by itself, only reading less output afterward does; (2) 'AI as decision-maker' and 'token-cost optimization' are two separate axes — building more callable functions reduces the cost of mechanical work (placement, validation, rebuilding), but no function can absorb genuine semantic judgment, and conflating the two risks either wrongly trying to eliminate necessary judgment or wrongly assuming judgment is free to automate away.
lesson: When asked whether an operation could be made cheaper, first ask whether the cost is coming from the mechanics (which a deterministic function already handles or could handle) or from the judgment (which cannot be delegated to a function no matter how it's built). Only the mechanical half is genuinely optimizable via better functions or a 'blueprint' library; conflating the two risks either overengineering functions that can't do the actual job, or wrongly assuming a judgment step could be skipped for a token discount. A future instance asked a similar cost-optimization question should separate 'is this mechanical or judgment' before proposing an architecture change.
impact: No code change. Standing clarification for future sessions on where token-cost optimization genuinely applies in this repo's tooling (mechanical execution — already handled by lib/*.js's pure functions and the content-intents pipeline) versus where a semantic judgment step is unavoidable and shouldn't be assumed away.
